quoted.text:
What happens next to #Oil markets after Saudi, UAE, Kuwait, Iraq etc finish stop heavily discounting oil so they can reduce the big build up in inventories?

"Middle Eastern producers have built up such large inventories that they are now desperate to sell their oil..... at the same time, there are difficulties getting tankers through the Strait of Hormuz because many shipowners are still unwilling to take the risk. As a result, producers are heavily discounting their crude, and oil prices are collapsing... The consequences of a blockade are ultimately quite complex - some rather unexpected and striking developments are taking place at the moment"  TotalEnergies CEO @PPouyanne 

Thx  @FrancoisDeBeaup

quoted.text:
Just a few weeks into the war, one of the Persian Gulf’s top oil producers quietly began sneaking its crude out of the Strait of Hormuz. Before long, the covert project became so successful that the United Arab Emirates was already approaching its pre-war rate of flows through the waterway by the time the US and Iran signed their interim peace deal.

The UAE’s aggressive push to get barrels safely out of the strait relied on tactics normally associated with sanctioned countries like Iran, Russia and Venezuela: the ships traveled “dark” without their transponders (and often under the cover of literal darkness) before offloading their cargo into other tankers waiting outside the waterway, and then returning back to collect more.
#oott https://t.co/soAumKEkOp

text:
Speculators are almost all on the same side of the boat. 

Brent net longs are near record lows while shorts hover near record highs. 

Crowded trades can persist—but they rarely end quietly.

 #Oil #OOTT #Brent #EnergyMarkets #Macro

quoted.text:
Managed money accounts continued to reduce bullish Brent crude bets in the week to 30 June, cutting the net long by 38% to near a historic low of just 55.6k contracts, down around 87% from a March peak of 429k. With the gross short hovering near an all-time high at 226k contracts, the long-short ratio has slumped to just 1.2 longs per short, also a historically low level that in the past has often proved unsustainable and triggered sharp bursts of position adjustment.

For now, however, the wave of supply being released from the Persian Gulf remains the key focus, with a major shift in the technical and/or fundamental outlook likely needed to rock an increasingly crowded boat of short sellers.
quoted.author: Ole_S_Hansen

SAUDI ARABIA SLASHES OIL PRICE TO ASIA

Saudi Arabia cut the August price of its flagship Arab Light crude for Asia by $11 a barrel—the biggest reduction in at least 26 years.

The move reflects weaker oil markets, rising global supply and intensifying competition, as Gulf producers ramp up exports following the easing of disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz.
